verbatim_evidence: First is EV transition risk. We've committed heavily to electrification — new platforms, battery supply agreements, retooling three plants — and the market isn't moving at the pace we planned for.
Consumer adoption in our core markets has slowed significantly.
We're now having internal conversations about whether to slow the transition roadmap which creates its own set of risks with regulators and investors.
stranded asset risk on ICE
we have manufacturing plants, tooling, and supply agreements built around internal combustion engines that may have a shorter useful life than their book value implies
we haven't done a formal impairment assessment on that scenario yet

The 25% tariffs on auto imports that came into effect have directly hit our cost structure.

Tariffs are the obvious one.
either broader US tariffs or retaliatory measures from the EU or China — would hit us hard and fast.
a sudden tariff announcement

verbatim_evidence: Battery cells are our most acute concentration risk right now.
That single event fundamentally changed how we think about supply chain concentration.
If any of those suppliers had a major quality or financial event the impact on our vehicle programmes would be immediate.
a battery supplier disruption due to geopolitical event
It reinforced how exposed we are through our supply chain's cyber posture, not just our own.
So our lithium concentration risk is actually one level further removed — it's their suppliers we're exposed to, not just them directly.
We're trying to rebalance the ownership structure and governance to protect ourselves better but the negotiation is complex and there's a real risk of disruption to operations during the transition.
Battery raw material prices — specifically lithium and cobalt — are volatile and tied to mining output, Chinese processing capacity, and demand from multiple industries competing for the same supply.
the geopolitical dimension of that concentration is something we're actively trying to reduce

verbatim_evidence: Fourth is software and technology risk. Modern vehicles are essentially software platforms now.
Our capability to develop, update, and secure vehicle software is lagging behind pure-play EV competitors and that gap is becoming a competitive risk.
We also have cybersecurity exposure in connected vehicles that we're actively working on but it's not where it needs to be.
AI liability in vehicles
the legal and liability framework for when those systems fail is genuinely unclear
we're accumulating liability exposure we can't fully quantify yet
The capital commitment is large and the technology risk — cell chemistry is still evolving — means we could be investing in a chemistry that's partially obsolete in five years.
Tesla's software and over-the-air update model has set a consumer expectation that we're still catching up to
Customers now expect vehicles to improve after purchase and our ability to deliver that is still limited
And our internal software development capability — we're trying to build it but we're competing with Google, Amazon, and Apple for the same talent.
The gap between where we need to be on software and where we are today is a genuine strategic risk.

verbatim_evidence: Seventh I'd add regulatory compliance — specifically emissions regulations. Euro 7 standards, the US EPA rules, and equivalent standards in other markets are all tightening.
Our ICE vehicle lineup has a defined compliance window and managing that transition without stranding assets is genuinely complex.
regulatory compliance
we will always be one of the most regulated industries in the world, whether it's safety standards, emissions, or increasingly software and data regulations
The new emissions standards coming into effect create a very tight compliance window for our remaining ICE models.
The cost of compliance per vehicle is significant and we're having to decide which models to carry forward and which to discontinue.
US CAFE standards and EPA emissions rules — the regulatory posture has shifted and we're watching that carefully in terms of what it means for our North American ICE lineup.
And the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive — CSRD — is imposing significant new disclosure obligations on us.
Our data infrastructure isn't ready for the granularity of reporting required.
Product safety — strong controls.
